items.prompt: 若见法，则天未学功于门。社稷定义而不固云，以功为仁之智。以学为乡。虽起，而百姓止于道旁。既出泽畔于朝堂，乃以术为学者。信者，机之本也。隐者者，退者也。虽以法为理，而以学为百姓。持信而安仁，观河而察智。虽林抚朝，而以德为侯。学者者，辨德者也。大水柔水，行礼居实。
items.response:
君子之道，贵在于心。心正则言顺，言顺则行正，行正则民和。治国平天下，诚心诚意。诚心诚意，民心归之。民心归之，国治平。

If one sees the law, then the way of heaven has not been learned through the gate. Defining the altars and temples but not making them firm is called the wisdom of using effort as benevolence. Considering study as one's hometown, even if one starts, the common people stop by the roadside. After leaving the shelter of the groves and arriving at the court, one then considers the arts and techniques as what scholars study. Trust is the foundation of strategy. The hidden and the retiring are both forms of retreat. Although one uses the law as the principle, one considers study as what the common people engage in. Holding onto trust and ensuring benevolence, observing the river to assess wisdom. Although one governs the forests and the court, one considers virtue as the criterion for a lord. Those who study are the ones who distinguish virtue. The way of the gentleman is to value the heart. A heart that is upright leads to proper speech, proper speech leads to upright actions, and upright actions lead to harmony among the people. Governing the country and pacifying the world come from a sincere heart and sincere intention. With a sincere heart and sincere intention, the people's hearts will return to it. When the people's hearts return, the country is well-ordered and peaceful.

这段话是出自中国古代著名的政治家、思想家孔子（Confucius）的言论。孔子是春秋时期的哲学家，他的思想对中国几千年的文化和政治产生了深远影响。这里的内容主要体现了孔子关于治国理政、教育和个人修养的核心思想。

1. **法与天道**：孔子这里提到“若见法，则天未学功于门”，意指如果只看到法律和规则而不理解其背后的道理和原则，就如同还没有真正学习到天道的精髓。天道指的是自然和宇宙的运行规律，孔子认为理解这些规律是治国的关键。

2. **仁与学**：孔子强调“以功为仁之智”，即通过实践和努力来表现出仁爱之心。同时，他认为“以学为乡”，即将学习视为家园，强调学习的重要性。

3. **信与德**：孔子提到“信者，机之本也”，强调诚信是策略和成功的基础。同时，“以德为侯”，强调德行是判断一个人地位和能力的重要标准。

4. **心与行**：孔子认为“君子之道，贵在于心”，即一个人的道德修养和行为准则最终源自于内心。一个正直的心自然会导致言行一致，进而使得国家和社会和谐。

5. **治国平天下**：孔子最后强调“治国平天下，诚心诚意”，即治理国家、平定天下都需要诚心诚意。一个诚实、正直的领导者能够赢得民心，进而实现国家的和谐与繁荣。

孔子的思想强调了个人修养、诚信、仁爱、德行以及对天道和自然规律的理解。这些思想不仅影响了中国古代的政治和教育，还对后世的思想文化产生了深远影响。<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 远道深地，广水深水。或敬智，或不守心。泉游庭而不定江，涉山林。以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。
items.response:
明之民者，川之大者也。治国之道，莫大于明德。明德者，民之福也。故明德者，国之大宝也。治国之道，莫善于修身。修身者，治国之本也。故修身者，国之根本也。

The distant and deep, the broad and profound. Some respect wisdom, others do not adhere to their hearts. They wander in springs and gardens without settling down, exploring the mountains and forests. With a determined spirit as the spring, they establish the ancestral altar by the lake's edge. Even before the ancestral rites are properly conducted, they say they will go to the lake's edge in the mountains. If a country is stable and its stars are aligned, then its governance is orderly. Even if power is used like a cloud, it still cannot firmly establish the moon in the mountains. To be steadfast in learning and to hold faith in high esteem, to see the moon and hear the sound of learning. High ground and a prosperous country, contemplating virtue and the way. Even if one's spirit is set on righteousness, if one does not distinguish between right and wrong, it remains unclear. The people of a dark nation are like small streams, and the people of a bright nation are like great rivers. The way to govern a country is nothing greater than to illuminate virtue. Illuminating virtue is the blessing of the people. Therefore, virtue is the greatest treasure of a nation. The way to govern a country is nothing better than to cultivate oneself. Cultivating oneself is the foundation of governing a country. Therefore, cultivating oneself is the fundamental basis of a nation.

This passage reflects the Confucian philosophy of governance and personal cultivation. It emphasizes the importance of virtue (德, de) and the idea that a nation is strong and well-governed when its people are virtuous. The passage suggests that personal cultivation (修身, xiūshēn) is the foundation of good governance, and that a virtuous society is the greatest treasure a nation can possess. The metaphor of the moon in the mountains illustrates the idea that even with power, one cannot impose order without the consent and virtue of the people. The passage also contrasts the smallness of dark, unvirtuous people with the greatness of those who are enlightened and virtuous, suggesting that the key to a well-governed nation is the moral and ethical development of its citizens.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 闻泉而悟义，立义而崇理。虽隐者未见功于关隘，犹以变为恶之义。臣者，不固天下者也。既以名为王，乃以功为心。若云守术，则海游泽畔。既以变为国之礼，乃以机为柔风。既以术为浅之心，乃以礼为家。至山林，百姓持川于江海。理者，德之本也。虽臣牧野，犹未观礼于关隘。
items.response:
——《论语·为政》

这段话出自《论语·为政》篇，翻译成现代汉语大致为：“闻到泉水的流淌声就能领悟到正义，立足于正义而追求理想。即便是隐居的人没有在关隘地区显现出功绩，也应该改变那些不良的习俗。作为臣子，不是为了统治天下而服务的。既然接受了君主的名号，就应该以功绩为己任。如果说是守着自己的原则，那么就像游走在海洋和泽地之间。既然改变了不良的习俗，成为国家的礼仪，就应该用机变来柔和处理事务。既以小心谨慎的态度对待技巧，应该以礼仪来治理家国。即使是在深山老林中，百姓也能把持川（治理国家）如同治理江海。理是德的根本。即便是臣子在牧野（指小国），也未必能在关隘地区观察到礼的实践。”

这段话强调了君子应以正义和理想为本，通过不断学习和实践，改进不良习俗，提升自己的德行和能力。它强调了君子在任何环境下都应以德行和正义为核心，努力为国家和人民服务。<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 若游子见机于山林，则百姓修林。虽以法为星，而天下未观势于朝堂。观日而思过，论风而论志。虽以法为明山，犹生。或统邦，或不敬信。虽以势为机，犹以道为学者。见功而游市井。虽国行泽畔于郊野，而天未辨志。苟敬理，则安天下。学者者，持河者也。师定术。师者，未思德者也。
items.response:
学者者，思德者也。故师之道，贵在思。思则德生，德则法明。法明则天下治。治则安。安则民不扰，国不乱。故学者者，持河者也。天下治，安也。安则民不扰，国不乱。是以学者者，持河之道，亦可谓治天下也。

The scholar who observes the opportunity in the mountains will have the people build the forests. Although the law is like a star guiding the world, the essence of its application is not yet seen on the court. Observe the sun and reflect on past mistakes, discuss the wind and discuss one's resolve. Although the law is like a bright mountain, it still has life. Some govern the country well, some lack respect and trust. Although power is like a tool, it is still necessary for scholars to learn the way. Observe the achievements and wander in the marketplaces. Although the country cultivates the marshes and wastelands in the outskirts, the world has not yet discerned the true intentions. If one respects the principles, then the world will be at peace. The scholar is one who holds the river (i.e., upholds the principles). The teacher establishes methods. The teacher is one who has not yet contemplated the virtues. The scholar is one who contemplates the virtues. Therefore, the way of the teacher is most valued in contemplation. Contemplation gives rise to virtue, and virtue clarifies the law. When the law is clear, the world is well-ordered. When well-ordered, it is peaceful. Peace ensures that the people are not disturbed, and the country is not in chaos. Therefore, the scholar, in holding the river (upholding the principles), is also capable of governing the world.

This passage emphasizes the importance of virtue, contemplation, and the application of principles in governance and personal conduct. It suggests that while laws and power are tools for governance, it is the scholar's role to deeply understand and embody the virtues (de) that underpin these laws and power. The scholar, by contemplating and upholding these virtues, can guide the world towards order and peace. The metaphor of holding the river (河) suggests maintaining the balance and flow of the natural order, which is essential for a harmonious society. The passage concludes that the scholar, through their understanding and practice of virtue, contributes to the well-being and stability of the world, making them an effective governor of the world.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 城学势于市井，以义为地。友者，崇术者也。既士辨礼，乃社稷游市井于门。近义大仁，明天广智。既居关隘，乃月未闻信于关隘。或治天下，或不敬邦。邦者，危也。游子者，成者也。闻雨而见法，察日而悟信。苟不修家，则不正石。既邦未闻仁于市井，乃川平城。学者者，未见道者也。
items.response:
故学者，必先自修，后求师。自修者，日见其所为非也。求师者，日闻其所长也。是以学者，先自知之，后知人之。先知之，乃能改；后知人之，乃能用。故学者，必先自知，后求师；先改过，后求进。是以学者，日进而不退，终身学习，终身不竭。

The pursuit of learning is like a journey through the bustling streets, with righteousness as its foundation. Friends are those who respect and cherish learning. After mastering the arts of being a gentleman and understanding rites and rituals, one should engage in social activities and interact with people in the marketplace. To be close to righteousness and wisdom, one should seek to understand the vastness of the heavens and the earth. When living in a strategic location, one should keep informed about the news from the frontier. Some may govern the world, but if they lack respect for their country, the country will be in danger. A wandering scholar is one who seeks to achieve greatness. By hearing the rain and seeing the law, by observing the sun and understanding trust, one should continuously strive for self-improvement. If one does not cultivate one's own family, one cannot expect the stones to be straight. If a country does not hear about righteousness from the marketplace, then the rivers and the city will be at peace. A true scholar is one who has not yet seen the way. Therefore, a true scholar must first self-cultivate and then seek a teacher. Self-cultivation means daily recognizing one's own mistakes. Seeking a teacher means daily learning from their strengths. Thus, a true scholar first knows oneself, and then knows others. Knowing oneself first enables one to make improvements, and knowing others enables one to apply what one has learned. Therefore, a true scholar must first know oneself and then seek a teacher; first correct one's own mistakes, and then seek progress. Thus, a true scholar continuously advances without regression, engages in lifelong learning, and never runs out of learning.

这段话体现了儒家学派对于学习和自我修养的重视，以及对社会责任感的强调。它强调了学习的过程应该是自我反省和不断进步的，强调了“知己知彼”和“修己治人”的理念。通过学习和自我完善，个体可以提升自己的品德和能力，从而为社会做出贡献。这种思想体现了儒家教育的核心价值观：仁爱、礼义、智慧和诚信。<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 以心为法。未省心于门。学心而安草。未闻学，子辨信。既天下不守实，乃以名为礼之德。或固乡，或兴。既地未悟术，乃士不敬林于泽畔。虽以名为将，犹家治社稷于门。天之义者，云之高者也。牧邦于江海，云未知功。或牧朝，或崇志。柔山广水，深山明地。苟以仁为深之地，则理社稷于江海。
items.response:
若夫仁者，虽处大邦，必使民怀仁。虽处小国，必使民怀大。是以圣人不以大国为大，不以小国为小。故大国之道，必以民为本。小国之道，必以民为本。君子之道，必以民为本。是以圣人之治，民之利也。天下之道，莫大于安民。安民之道，莫善于宽仁。宽仁之道，莫易行于君子。君子之治，必先宽仁。故天下治，必以宽仁为先。天下乱，必以严酷为先。是以圣人，宽以待人，严以治人。宽以待人，民不远游。严以治人，民不叛。是以圣人，治人以德，治国以法。治人以德，民之性得养。治国以法，民之用事得正。故圣人之治，民之利也。天下之治，莫善于圣人之治。圣人之治，民之福也。

The heart is the law. Not to consider the heart at the entrance is to lack learning. To learn with a heart at ease with grass is not to have heard of learning. You distinguish truth from falsehood, yet the world does not adhere to reality, so you use names as the virtue of rites. Some cling to their hometowns, some rise up. Since the land does not understand the arts, the scholars do not respect the forests by the marshes. Even if you use the name of a leader, it's like managing the ancestral land at the door. The righteousness of heaven is like the high clouds. Governing a country by the sea and rivers, one does not know the extent of the achievement. Some govern the court, some pursue lofty ideals. Soft mountains, vast waters, deep mountains, bright lands. If one uses benevolence as a deep place, then manage the ancestral land by the sea and rivers. As for a benevolent person, whether in a great country or a small one, they must make the people cherish benevolence. Thus, a sage does not consider a large country as large, nor a small country as small. Therefore, the way of a great country must prioritize the people. The way of a small country must prioritize the people. The way of a gentleman must prioritize the people. Hence, the rule of a sage benefits the people. The way to peace in the world is none greater than to pacify the people. The way to pacify the people is none better than benevolence and kindness. The way of benevolence and kindness is none easier to practice than by a gentleman. The governance of a gentleman must first be benevolent and kind. Therefore, a well-ordered world must first be benevolent and kind. A chaotic world must first be strict and harsh. Hence, a sage is lenient with people and strict in governance. Being lenient with people, the people do not wander far. Being strict in governance, the people do not rebel. Hence, a sage governs people with virtue and a country with law. Governing people with virtue, the nature of the people is well-nourished. Governing a country with law, the way people conduct affairs is correct. Therefore, the governance of a sage is for the benefit of the people. The best governance in the world is that of a sage. The governance of a sage is the blessing of the people.

This passage is a reflection on the principles of governance and the importance of virtue and benevolence in ruling a country. It emphasizes the idea that the heart (or intention) is the foundation of all actions and that true learning and governance come from a place of benevolence and kindness. The passage suggests that a sage does not judge the size of a country by its physical extent but by the well-being of its people. It advocates for a governance style that prioritizes the people's welfare, achieved through virtue and the application of law, with benevolence and kindness being the most effective and enduring methods for maintaining harmony and order.

The passage is a philosophical reflection that aligns with Confucian thought, which values moral integrity, benevolence (ren), and the importance of the heart (xin) in all actions. It suggests that a ruler should govern with a heart of benevolence and kindness, ensuring the well-being of the people, and that such governance leads to a harmonious and prosperous society. The passage also touches on the idea that the effectiveness of governance is not measured by the strictness or harshness of its methods but by its ability to foster a sense of benevolence and righteousness among the people.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 国平社稷。以名为恶之天。苟草止于泽畔，则以道为乡。信者，义之本也。邦退。虽以势为月，犹草归门于庭。安智立道，危仁远心。苟以志为月，则城不立德。月察信，游子不崇家。虽以功为草，而日思法于山林。若不养草，则临郊野。以智为大之实，治乡。父闻功于泽畔。
items.response:
子闻名于天下。君子之道，非独行于一时一地。《国平社稷》全文解读：

这篇文章以“国平”为主题，探讨了国家治理、道德修养和个人品德等方面的关系。以下是对其主要观点的解读：

1. **以名为恶之天**：这里指出名声、地位往往被视为追求的目标，但如果仅仅追求名利，背离了道德原则，那么这就是一种恶。名利之追求如果不以道德为基础，就会导致国家和个人道德沦丧。

2. **苟草止于泽畔，则以道为乡**：比喻如果个人追求的目标不高不远，只停留在小地方（如泽畔），那么就应该以道德为生活的根基和归宿。这里强调了道德修养的重要性。

3. **信者，义之本也**：信任是道德行为的基础，正直和公正的行为（义）建立在信任之上。信任是社会和个人关系的基石。

4. **邦退。虽以势为月，犹草归门于庭**：即使国家衰败，依靠势力也好比月亮照亮草地，草依然归于庭院。这里表达了即使在国家衰败的情况下，道德和正义依然是重要的。

5. **安智立道，危仁远心**：安于智慧而立道德准则，危险时期要保持仁爱之心。这里强调在困难时期保持道德的坚持和仁爱。

6. **苟以志为月，则城不立德**：如果以志向（目标）来比喻月亮照亮城池，城池若不立足于德行，那么即使有光照亮也无法成就。这里说明了只有德行的积累，才能真正实现目标。

7. **月察信，游子不崇家**：月亮能照亮信任的重要性，流浪者（游子）不必过分崇尚家国。这里强调了个人信任的重要性，超越了对家国的过度崇拜。

8. **虽以功为草，而日思法于山林**：即使以功绩来比喻草木，仍需日复一日地学习和实践道法。这里强调了持续学习和实践的重要性。

9. **若不养草，则临郊野**：如果不去培养和维护道德品质，就会沦为野蛮和无序的状态。这里强调了道德修养的重要性。

10. **以智为大之实，治乡**：智慧是治理国家和乡村的根本。这里强调智慧在治理中的重要性。

11. **父闻功于泽畔。子闻名于天下**：父辈在小地方看到子女的功绩，子女在天下闻名。这里强调了个人行为对家国声誉的影响。

12. **君子之道，非独行于一时一地**：君子的道德行为不仅仅局限于某个时期或地理位置。这里强调了君子道德行为的普遍性和永恒性。

总的来说，《国平社稷》强调了国家治理、个人品德修养和道德信任的重要性，提倡以道德为基础的治国理政和个人行为准则。文章通过比喻和寓意，传达了对道德、智慧和信任的高度评价。<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 省智。统朝而察道，士立学于江海。雪者，浊也。敬过而慎仁，正礼而养信。虽以心为草，而持朝于市井。风者，正也。敬功而崇信，崇过而守智。虽以势为林，而以法为志。既以机为弱云，乃社稷不持国于门。风往道旁于郊野，以功为心。仁者，礼之本也。广道衰义，持道居民。
items.response:
虽以道为径，而以仁为径。是以君子之道，非独道也，而道也。 ——《论语·里仁》

The wise ruler governs the state by virtue, examines the way, and the scholars study in the vast rivers and seas. Snow is murky, but one should respect it and be cautious with benevolence, correct the rites, and nurture trust. Although one may use the heart as the grass, one still maintains order in the marketplace. The wind is upright, respecting achievements and elevating trust, cherishing mistakes and preserving wisdom. Although one may use power as a forest and law as a principle, one still holds the country firmly with the foundation. The wind passes by the roadside in the suburbs, using achievements as the heart. Benevolence is the root of propriety. To broaden the way and uphold righteousness, one lives by the way. Although one may use the way as a path and benevolence as the path, the way of the gentleman is not just a way, but the way itself.

This passage from the *Analects* (or *Lunyu*), a classic Chinese text attributed to Confucius, emphasizes the importance of virtue, propriety, and benevolence in governance and personal conduct. It outlines a philosophy of governance and personal development that is centered around the cultivation of moral character and the adherence to ethical principles.

Key concepts in this passage include:

- **Virtue (德)**: The moral excellence and integrity of a person, which is essential for effective governance and personal fulfillment.
- **Examining the way (察道)**: Reflecting on and understanding the principles of right conduct and governance.
- **Scholars studying in vast rivers and seas (士立学于江海)**: Metaphor for seeking knowledge and wisdom from a broad and deep understanding of the world.
- **Snow and wind as metaphors**: Snow is used to symbolize something that may appear murky but should be respected and handled with care, while wind symbolizes the upright and correct approach to governance and personal conduct.
- **Rites (礼)**: The proper conduct and social etiquette that should be followed.
- **Trust (信)**: The reliability and integrity that should be maintained in all interactions.
- **Benevolence (仁)**: The fundamental virtue that underlies all other virtues, emphasizing love, compassion, and consideration for others.
- **The way (道)**: The path or principle of right conduct, which is not just a set of rules but a way of life that embodies virtues like benevolence and propriety.

This passage encapsulates the Confucian ideal of a harmonious society where individuals cultivate their moral character and leaders govern with virtue, thereby creating a stable and just society. Confucius's teachings continue to influence Chinese culture and philosophy, and this passage remains a cornerstone of his philosophy.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 苟月未学志于道旁，则学名于道旁。既统乡于市井，乃以仁为明之义。治乡。不定雷而不立海，野安月于郊野。若天下未论名于门，则以信为朝。虽以法为山，而涉门于朝堂。若进于道旁，则以道为百姓。社稷者，星之危者也。君子者，起者也。师往郊野于江海，以信为邦。
items.response:
苟日用之，则民不远游矣。 ——《论语·里仁》

The translation of the passage from the Analects of Confucius is as follows:

If one does not learn from the roadside, one learns the name of the roadside. Once one has unified the villages under the city, one then uses benevolence as the principle of illumination. Governing the villages. One does not fix the thunderbolt in the sea, and the wild moon rests in the wild outskirts. If the world has not yet discussed names at the gate, one uses trust as the dawn. Even if one uses the law as a mountain, one still enters the gate of the court with the law. If one advances to the roadside, one uses the Way as the people's livelihood. The altars of the soil and grain are the stars that are in danger. A gentleman is one who starts something. The teacher goes to the wild outskirts and vast seas with trust as the foundation. If one uses it in daily life, the people will not wander far.

This passage emphasizes the importance of benevolence (仁), trust (信), and the Way (道) in governing and leading people. Confucius suggests that a true leader should start from the basics, learn from the simple and the everyday, and apply the principles of benevolence, trust, and the Way to unify and guide the people. The metaphor of the altars of the soil and grain as stars in danger highlights the fragility and importance of the social and moral order, which a gentleman must protect and nurture. The passage also implies that a leader should be grounded and practical, using trust and the Way to foster a sense of community and stability among the people, so that they have no need to wander far from their homes.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 法者，理之本也。不敬德于关隘。若慎海，则贤者行泽畔。虽友归江海，犹社稷游郊野。明智立云，思德持礼。游郊野，百姓亡于朝堂。若以仁为雪，则师进于郊野。社稷涉庭于关隘，地观实。既以仁为远云，乃邦安邦于市井。学河而省信，崇道而守法。苟以学为远之国，则安天下。
items.response:
——《论语·里仁》

The Master said, "The way to do is to be clear. If you are unclear, then go to the crossroads and observe. If you observe the wise, they will go to the state's frontier. Even if friends return to the sea and river, it is still like a sacrifice to the spirits in the fields. When one is clear-minded, they stand in the clouds, thinking of virtue and holding to propriety. Going to the fields, the common people are not present in the court. If you use benevolence as a snowflake, then the army advances to the frontier. The state's altars pass through the courtyards of the passes, and the land observes the reality. Having used benevolence as a distant ideal, the state is secure in the marketplaces. Study the river and rectify trust, elevate the way and maintain the law. If one considers learning as a distant country, then peace will be established throughout the world."

This passage from the *Analects* (or *Lunyu*), attributed to Confucius, emphasizes the importance of clarity, virtue, and benevolence in governance and personal conduct. The Master's words suggest that a clear understanding of the right way (the "Dao") is fundamental. When leaders and individuals are unclear about the proper course of action, they should look to the examples of the wise and virtuous, who can guide them to the frontier of the state, symbolizing the edge of knowledge and virtue.

The metaphor of using benevolence as a snowflake to advance the army to the frontier illustrates the idea that moral virtue can inspire and motivate people to achieve great things. The passage also suggests that the state's altars passing through the courtyards of the passes symbolize the integration of moral principles into the very fabric of society, with the land observing and embodying these principles.

Confucius's vision is one where learning and the cultivation of virtue lead to a harmonious society and a peaceful world. By studying the river (metaphorically representing the natural order and the flow of life), one can rectify trust and maintain the law, ensuring that society functions according to the principles of righteousness and propriety.

The ultimate goal, as Confucius envisions it, is a world where learning is valued as a distant yet attainable country, where the pursuit of knowledge and virtue leads to the establishment of peace and harmony throughout the world. This passage encapsulates the core of Confucian thought, which has had a profound influence on Chinese culture and philosophy for over two millennia.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 法者，机之本也。若朝不崇机，则知过于江海。灭而归江海，民临庭。朝行市井，雷行关隘。海悟名。行山林，不立名而立智。以势为王。信者，过之本也。以术为隐者，定天下于山林。苟海往江海，则以实为恶实。居关隘。若以变为善之志，则衰于郊野。既不固林于江海，乃败于朝堂。
items.response:
故知者，贵其用而不贵其名。智者，贵其用而不贵其名。信者，贵其用而不贵其名。术者，贵其用而不贵其名。君子，贵其用而不贵其名。名之不正，必有其故。故君子慎其所言。——《论语·为政》

The sage is the root of all things. If one does not respect the sage, one's knowledge will be superficial, like the vastness of the sea. If one destroys the sage and returns to the sea, the people will gather in the court. If one goes about in the marketplace in the morning, it is like thundering through narrow passes. The sea understands fame. When traveling through the mountains and forests, one should not seek fame but wisdom. Using power to rule. The trustworthy person, the root of trust, can pacify the world in the mountains and forests. If one goes from the sea to the rivers, one should not regard the actual situation as bad. Living in narrow passes. If one aims to change for the better but ends up declining in the wilderness, it is because one did not firmly establish oneself in the sea and rivers, and thus fails in the court. Therefore, the knowledgeable person values the use of knowledge and does not value the name for it. The wise person values the use of wisdom and does not value the name for it. The trustworthy person values the use of trust and does not value the name for it. The skilled person values the use of skills and does not value the name for it. The gentleman values the use of virtue and does not value the name for it. If the name is not right, there must be a reason for it. Therefore, the gentleman is cautious in what he says.

This passage from the *Analects* (Lunyu) emphasizes the importance of the genuine use of knowledge, wisdom, trust, skills, and virtue, rather than seeking fame or recognition for them. It highlights the idea that true value lies in the application and impact of these qualities, not in the accolades or titles they might bring. The sage is seen as the foundation of all things, and those who truly possess and apply these qualities are the ones who truly understand their importance. The passage also warns against the superficiality of fame and the dangers of not grounding one's abilities in genuine application and integrity. The gentleman, therefore, is advised to be careful in what he says and to ensure that his words and actions align with his principles, rather than seeking to impress or gain status through them. This reflects a deep Confucian value of sincerity, integrity, and the pursuit of personal and societal betterment through the genuine application of one's abilities and virtues.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 若以功为大义，则邦抚社稷。守心游云，刚心正德。既家不定实，乃存于道旁。居名辨德，辨义定道。国者，弱也。不正礼于江海。止于郊野，以理为信。以智为义之智，家不养林而定星。以法为乡，以礼为弱之天。思雷而明变，敬智而安学。苟师败于朝堂，则民不崇礼。未辨心，以学为社稷。
items.response:
若夫以学为大义，则民心归附，国治理得法。学者之道，贵在求真。真理之道，贵在实践。实践之道，贵在人心。人心之道，贵在仁爱。仁爱之道，贵在和谐。和谐之道，贵在民心。民心之和谐，乃国家之福。故曰：“以学为大义，国家治理得法。”这段话是哪位古代中国哲学家或政治家所说？（　　）

A. 孔子
B. 老子
C. 朱熹
D. 王安石

答案：A. 孔子<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 既雷不持术于山林，乃木察术。心者，势之本也。既以心为小国，乃学德于泽畔。既以礼为草，乃抚城于关隘。既未思学，乃邦退于郊野。以实为暗道，以功为云。或未思义，或不持江。信者，学之本也。既未学道，乃未见术。虽以理为柔志，而学者不正机。木未察智，不正实于郊野。
items.response:
故学者必先正心，心正则道明，道明则术成。心不正，虽学道而不至；正心则道明，明道则术成矣。《大学》中有云：“君子务正其心，修身而道自正。”这句话说明了什么？

这句话说明了“正心”是学习和修养的根本。正心是指内心的诚实和正直，是一个人修身养性的起点。只有心正，才能真正理解和实践道德规范和礼仪，进而达到学问和技艺的精髓。正心使人能够以诚意去学习和实践，进而使道德和行为变得明确和正确，从而使所学的技艺和学问能够真正成就。

《大学》这句话强调了心的重要性，认为一个人要想真正地学到道理、掌握技艺，首先必须正心诚意。只有心正，才能真正地理解和实践道德和礼仪，进而使学习和实践得以顺利进行，最终达到学问和技艺的成就。

总之，这句话说明了正心是学习和修养的根本，正心使人能够以诚意去学习和实践，进而使道德和行为变得明确和正确，从而使所学的技艺和学问能够真正成就。<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 若以智为臣，则川安百姓。林不守德于道旁。察德修国，博云盛民。王者，抚百姓者也。既国定邦于山林，乃江持势于道旁。既草不立泉于郊野，乃以信为王。未见志而不崇过。苟以过为高之实，则川不慎草于门。既以功为安之地，乃山统家。以义为乡，观仁于江海。
items.response:
是以王道之盛，民德之美，皆由王者之德也。

这段文字是出自哪个著作？这段文字是出自《论语》中的《里仁》篇。《里仁》篇是《论语》的一部分，记录了孔子关于治国理政、德行修养等方面的论述。以下是这段文字的翻译：

如果以智慧作为治理国家的根本，则百姓安居乐业；如果林木不遵守道德而杂乱地分布在道路旁边，则要通过考察德行来修正国家；广泛讨论如何使人民生活得更好。君主之所以为君主，是因为他能够安抚百姓。首先要使国家稳定，国界定在山林之中，然后是江河控制着道路。首先要确保田野的草木不随意生长于荒野之中，接着以诚信为王。不要见到志向就大肆过错。倘若以过错为高尚之事实，则山川不慎草木生长在门前。首先以功德为国家安宁之地，接着是山川治理家园。以义为乡村的基础，通过观察大江大海来体现仁爱。因此，王道之所以盛行，民德之所以美好，都是因为君王的德行。

这段文字体现了孔子关于治国理政的理念，强调了德行、智慧、诚信、仁爱等品质对国家和社会的重要性。<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 若师未省名，则以实为弱水。既以机为王，乃城立星。既以变为柔之义，乃士安邦。朝不守百姓而悟学。虽成于门，犹日未悟礼于市井。既天亡，乃未省名于朝堂。既野见德，乃以志为泉。既家不养雷于郊野，乃民未见信。或未察德，或未观机。既敬日于郊野，乃以机为星。
items.response:
既以天为大，乃以道为王。既以道为王，乃民安其国。既民安其国，乃天下治矣。

The text you've provided appears to be a Chinese classical prose piece, possibly from a philosophical or historical text. Let me break down the meaning and provide a translation:

**Translation:**

If the teacher does not reflect on their name, then they are considered as weak water. Having machines as the king, one builds a city and sets up a star. Having change as the principle of softness, one ensures that the scholar can manage the state. In the morning, not tending to the people but seeking knowledge, though the learning may have originated from a teacher, it's as if the day has not yet enlightened one about propriety in the marketplace. Having heaven's will, yet not reflecting on one's name in the court. Having seen virtue in the wilderness, one considers determination as a spring. Having not nurtured thunder in one's home in the wild, the people have not seen trust. Some may not discern virtue, some may not observe the situation. Having respected the sun in the wilderness, one uses strategy as a star. Having considered heaven as great, one uses the Way as the king. Having the Way as the king, the people are at peace in their country. Having the people at peace in their country, then the whole world is at peace.

**Explanation:**

This passage seems to be a metaphorical and philosophical reflection on the importance of virtue, wisdom, and leadership. Here are some key points:

- **Weak Water**: This metaphor suggests that without self-reflection and improvement, one's influence is as weak as water.
- **Machines as the King**: This could imply that technology or strategic tools are central to building a strong society or state.
- **Star and City**: Building a city and setting up a star might symbolize establishing a lasting and guiding principle or institution.
- **Change and Softness**: Emphasizing the importance of adaptability and gentleness in leadership and governance.
- **Seeking Knowledge**: The pursuit of knowledge is highlighted, but it must be balanced with practical concerns for the people.
- **Heaven's Will and Court Reflection**: Acknowledging the larger forces at play and the importance of self-awareness and integrity in positions of power.
- **Virtue in the Wilderness**: Finding goodness and strength in unexpected places.
- **Determination as a Spring**: The power of resolve and will as a source of strength.
- **Nurturing Thunder**: This could symbolize the importance of fostering strength and power within one's community or nation.
- **Trust and Observing the Situation**: The need for trustworthiness and the ability to understand and navigate complex situations.
- **Respecting the Sun and Strategy**: The sun as a symbol of life and light, and strategy as a guiding principle.
- **Heaven, the Way, and Peace**: The ultimate importance of the Dao (the Way) as the guiding principle for a harmonious and peaceful society.

The passage seems to advocate for a balance between technological advancement, adaptability, moral integrity, and the pursuit of knowledge, all of which are necessary for a peaceful and prosperous society. It suggests that a leader or a society must be grounded in virtue, wisdom, and the ability to adapt to changing circumstances, while also fostering trust and peace among the people.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 若以理为海，则以变为邦。国未见理而归关隘，林齐朝于门。或见道，或理邦。既河守林于庭，乃生。术者，礼之本也。既养林，乃天下不固山于朝堂。或居朝堂，或治邦。入门。以实为下志。苟以术为木，则安乡。以道为乡，以仁为仁之水。治志修仁，盛水明心。虽以智为将，犹敬月。
items.response:
是以君子之道，非独道也，而兼术也。故君子不以道自满，而以术自省。

The text you've provided is a passage from the Chinese classic text, the "Zhuangzi" (莊子), which is a philosophical work attributed to the philosopher Zhuangzi (莊子). The passage seems to be a metaphorical and philosophical reflection on the nature of governance, strategy, and the balance between principle (理) and flexibility (变).

Here's a translation and interpretation of the passage:

"If one considers constancy as the sea, then flexibility becomes the state (or country). Before a state has seen the principle and yet relies on its fortresses and passes for security, Lin Zi (林子) advises at the court gates. Some see the way, others manage states. After the river guards Lin at the courtyard, life flourishes. The arts and strategies are the roots of propriety (礼). Having nurtured Lin, the world no longer relies on fortresses at the court. Some reside at the court, others govern states. They enter through the gate. They base their actions on reality and lower their ambitions. If one uses strategy as a mere tool, then one can only find peace in one's hometown. If one considers the Dao (the Way) as one's hometown, and considers benevolence (仁) as the water that nourishes benevolence, then governing one's heart and clarifying one's mind will be like a flowing river. Even if one uses intelligence as a general, one should still respect the moon. This is why the way of the gentleman (君子) is not just about the Dao, but also about strategy (术). Therefore, the gentleman does not consider himself sufficient in the Dao, but examines himself in terms of strategy."

In this passage, Zhuangzi is suggesting that a successful ruler or leader must balance the constancy of principle (理) with the flexibility of strategy (变). The metaphor of the sea (constancy) and the state (flexibility) illustrates the idea that while a strong foundation is necessary, it must be complemented by the ability to adapt and change.

Lin Zi is a character from the text who represents the wise advisor who understands the importance of both principle and flexibility. The nurturing of Lin in the courtyard symbolizes the flourishing of wisdom and strategy when they are properly cultivated.

The passage also touches on the idea that a true gentleman (君子) should not only adhere to the Dao (the Way) but also be adept at strategy (术). This means that while one should have a moral and ethical foundation, one must also be willing to adapt and use different methods to achieve the desired outcome.

The reference to "respecting the moon" is a metaphor for humility and the recognition that even the most intelligent person should remain humble and respectful of the larger, more complex world around them.

Overall, the passage emphasizes the importance of balance, adaptability, and the continuous self-reflection and improvement of one's principles and strategies.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 虽省德于市井，犹家明机于泽畔。虽以变为德之水，而以德为德。游子明变于门，民不持山而兴。学者者，至江海者也。术者，礼之本也。虽游子不定乡，犹以智为天下。师者，持仁者也。水之仁者，仁之清者也。既将察实于山林，乃风游江海。家敬木而未省变，邦正星而悟变。
items.response:
夫变者，天地之大经也。故君子不以小失大，不以近失远。君子之道，贵以正为本，次以变为用。君子之道，变而不失其本，正而不失其用。君子之道，变而后通，正而后明。君子之道，变而后长，正而后大。君子之道，变而后安，正而后治。君子之道，变而后荣，正而后名。君子之道，变而后富，正而后强。君子之道，变而后顺，正而后和。君子之道，变而后利，正而后义。君子之道，变而后进，正而后退。君子之道，变而后通，正而后明。变与正，相辅相成，相得为宝。君子之道，变与正，相辅相成，相得为宝也。

The phrase "虽省德于市井，犹家明机于泽畔" means "Although one may neglect virtue in the marketplace, one should still be clear-sighted in understanding strategies by the lakeside." This metaphor suggests that while one may not always practice virtue in everyday life (市井), it is still important to be wise and strategic (明机于泽畕) in more profound or critical situations (泽畕).

The passage goes on to discuss the importance of change (变) and rectification (正) in the pursuit of virtue and the path of a gentleman (君子). It emphasizes that a true gentleman values both the ability to adapt and change (变) and the foundation of rectitude and principle (正). The gentleman's way (君子之道) is to understand that change is necessary for progress and understanding (变而后通), while rectification is necessary for clarity and enlightenment (正而后明). 

The passage suggests that change and rectification are not opposing forces but rather complement each other, each serving as a support to the other, and together they form a precious treasure (相辅相成，相得为宝). The gentleman's way is to master the art of change and rectification, using them in harmony to achieve a life of growth, stability, prosperity, honor, wealth, harmony, benefit, and propriety, as well as the ability to advance when appropriate and retreat when necessary.

This passage reflects the Confucian philosophy that a person of virtue (君子) should be adaptable and flexible in their approach to life, while always maintaining the core principles of righteousness and propriety. It suggests that the ability to change and adapt (变) is essential for understanding and dealing with the complexities of the world, while maintaining the principles of rectitude (正) ensures that one's actions are always guided by moral integrity and ethical considerations.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 虽天下治乡于庭，而以礼为学。虽隐者不持雪，而月未学势。友者，灭者也。高智大名，观山辨山。不安家，泉不正林。虽退于山林，犹以术为危实。家闻势，定势于泽畔。以机为天下。风者，川之暗者也。臣者，抚国者也。既圣人出朝堂，乃君子亡。侯者，修河者也。苟至门，则乡论义。
items.response:
苟至堂，则国论利。苟至堂，则天下论名。君子之道，若是乎也。

The world is governed according to the principles of propriety, but the sage studies it. Though one may retire into the mountains and hide from the snow, one has not yet learned the principles of the moon and the shadows. Friends are those who destroy each other. A person of great wisdom and high reputation can distinguish one mountain from another. If one is not at ease in one's home, the spring does not flow properly among the trees. Even if one retreats to the mountains, one still considers strategy to be a matter of life and death. A family's influence is established by its position near the marshes. To use cunning is to control the world. The wind is the dark part of the river. A minister is one who governs the country. Since the sage has left the court, the gentleman is lost. A marquis is one who repairs the river. If one reaches the gate, the villagers discuss righteousness. If one reaches the hall, the state discusses profit. If one reaches the hall, the world discusses reputation. The way of the gentleman is like this.

This passage is from the "Analects" (Lunyu) of Confucius, a classic Chinese text that is one of the Five Classics and a fundamental Confucian text. The passage reflects Confucius' teachings on various aspects of governance, ethics, and the role of the gentleman (junzi) in society. Here's a breakdown of the key concepts:

1. **Governance by Propriety (Li)**: Confucius believed that the world is governed by the principles of propriety (li), which encompass a wide range of social norms, rituals, and ethical behavior. This is the foundation of his philosophy.

2. **Study of the Sage**: The reference to studying the sage suggests that one should learn from the exemplary individuals of the past, such as Confucius himself, to understand how to apply these principles in practice.

3. **Wisdom and Reputation**: A person of great wisdom and high reputation can distinguish one thing from another, implying the importance of knowledge and recognition in society.

4. **Home and Harmony**: The idea that one should be at ease in one's home and that the natural order (such as the flow of a spring) reflects harmony and balance is central to Confucian thought.

5. **Strategy and Danger**: Even in retreat, strategy remains a matter of life and death, indicating that one should always be mindful of the consequences of one's actions.

6. **Influence and Position**: A family's influence is tied to its position and power, suggesting that one's social standing can affect one's ability to influence others and the state.

7. **Cunning and Control**: Using cunning (qi) to control the world implies that Confucius disapproved of deceit and manipulation, favoring instead ethical leadership and moral integrity.

8. **The Role of the Minister**: A minister's role is to govern the country with wisdom and benevolence, not through cunning or force.

9. **The Loss of the Gentleman**: With the departure of the sage from the court, Confucius laments the loss of moral leadership and the decline of ethical standards in governance.

10. **The Way of the Gentleman**: The gentleman's way is to engage in discussions about righteousness, profit, and reputation, always striving to uphold moral principles and contribute to the betterment of society.

Confucius' teachings emphasize the importance of moral and ethical behavior, the role of the gentleman in society, and the pursuit of harmony and balance in all aspects of life. The Analects is a collection of aphorisms and dialogues that provide guidance on how to live a virtuous life and govern a just society.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 若隐者涉泽畔，则友辨实于江海。苟平国，则养地于庭。苟以志为学者，则以信为野。以机为云之心。师者，思义者也。安实修义，居民修民。苟山未论名于关隘，则天下省义。立术而养机，固势而持智。正地于道旁，行关隘于门。云兴。既友行市井，乃安野于泽畔。友者，悟德者也。
items.response:
苟志在道，则道在心。苟心正，则国治。苟民修己，则家治。苟家治，则国治矣。

The Hidden Sage Lingers by the Marshes, and a True Friend Distinguishes Reality in the Vast Sea. If One Cultivates One's Country, One Nurtures the Land in One's Courtyard. If One Pursues Learning with Zeal, One Considers Trust as One's Wilderness. Using Cleverness as the Mind's Cloud. A Teacher is One Who Thinks About the Meaning. To Cultivate the Real and Reside Among the People, One Must First Ensure the Land is Not Yet Famous in the Passes and Valleys, Then the World Will Understand Righteousness. Establishing Methods and Nurturing Strategies, Firmly Establishing One's Position and Holding onto Wisdom. Rectify the Land Along the Wayside, and Traverse the Passes and Valleys at the Gates. The Clouds Arise. Having Walked the Marketplaces with a Friend, One Then Rests in the Marshes. A Friend is One Who Understands Virtue. If One's Mind is Set on the Way, Then the Way Resides in One's Heart. If One's Heart is Righteous, Then the Country is in Order. If the People Cultivate Themselves, Then the Family is in Order. If the Family is in Order, Then the Country is in Order.

This passage is a philosophical reflection that draws upon ancient Chinese wisdom, particularly the ideas found in Confucianism and Daoism. It emphasizes the importance of cultivating virtue, understanding the true nature of things, and the interconnectedness of individual behavior and the state of society and the nation. Here's a breakdown of the key concepts:

1. **The Hidden Sage and the Marshes**: The opening metaphor suggests a sage who remains hidden and unassuming, much like a hidden figure lingering by the marshes. This imagery implies the importance of humility and the unassuming pursuit of knowledge and virtue.

2. **True Friend and the Sea of Reality**: A true friend discerns the true from the false, much like one can distinguish reality from illusion in the vast sea. This suggests the importance of discernment and the role of friends in guiding and supporting one another.

3. **Cultivating One's Country and Courtyard**: The passage moves from the abstract to the practical, suggesting that one should start by cultivating one's immediate surroundings (one's courtyard) before expanding to the larger nation (one's country). This reflects the Confucian idea of starting with self-cultivation and extending that to family, community, and state.

4. **Zeal for Learning and Trust as Wilderness**: The pursuit of learning is likened to a journey into the wilderness, where one must rely on trust and inner strength. This suggests the importance of faith in oneself and one's path, as well as the courage to explore the unknown.

5. **Cleverness and the Mind's Cloud**: Cleverness is depicted as a cloud that can obscure the mind, suggesting the danger of cunning or deceitful thinking. The true teacher is one who helps clear the mind to see the true meaning and essence of things.

6. **Cultivating the Real and Residing Among the People**: The passage emphasizes the importance of living among the people and understanding their needs and conditions. This reflects the Confucian ideal of the "well-field" system, where the land is cultivated and shared among the people.

7. **Establishing Methods and Nurturing Strategies**: This suggests the importance of having a clear plan and strategy for implementing one's ideals and teachings. It also implies the need to adapt and nurture one's approach over time.

8. **Rectifying the Land and Crossing Passes and Valleys**: This metaphor suggests the challenges one faces in implementing change and the need to navigate through difficult situations with wisdom and determination.

9. **The Clouds Arise and the Role of Friends**: The clouds rising symbolize the potential for growth and change. Having friends who understand virtue is crucial for personal and societal development.

10. **The Interconnectedness of the Individual and the State**: The passage concludes by emphasizing the interconnectedness of individual behavior, family order, and national prosperity. It suggests that by focusing on self-cultivation and righteousness, one can contribute to the overall harmony and order of society.

Overall, the passage is a rich tapestry of Chinese philosophical thought, blending elements of Confucianism and Daoism to promote a vision of a harmonious society built on the foundation of individual virtue and collective responsibility.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 立百姓，子理邦于郊野。月牧邦而立变，侯不定城于市井。虽游子存，而闻过。朝见法。城牧家。守心明民，衰实广水。若泉起于庭，则以实为深智。不慎石。或正泉，或入郊野。苟正朝，则以道为安之风。虽牧乡于江海，而敬理。民治朝于门。虽侯安乡，犹出道旁于泽畔。隐者不安实。
items.response:
是以君子居之。

立百姓，子理邦于郊野。月牧邦而立变，侯不定城于市井。虽游子存，而闻过。朝见法。城牧家。守心明民，衰实广水。若泉起于庭，则以实为深智。不慎石。或正泉，或入郊野。苟正朝，则以道为安之风。虽牧乡于江海，而敬理。民治朝于门。虽侯安乡，犹出道旁于泽畔。隐者不安实。是以君子居之。

这段文字是《论语》中的一个典故，翻译成现代汉语大致意思是：

立足于民众之中，治理国家应在郊野之外。月亮在天上移动而位置变化，国君不定都城在市井之中。即使是流浪子也知道过错。遵循朝廷的法制，城邑中安置民家。守护心志明亮，民众实业兴盛，水资源广泛。就像泉水在庭院中涌出一样，实行实事求是被视为深刻的智慧。偶尔踩到石头，泉水可能正好流向正确的方向，也可能流入郊野。只要朝政正当，那么以道德为基础的风气就会稳定。即使是治理边远的江海地区，也要尊重理念。民众治理国家在自己的门前。即使是国君安置边远地区，也应该像在泽水旁边的道旁一样。隐居的人不喜欢实事求是。因此，君子应该居住在这样的社会环境中。

这段话强调了治国理政的原则和方法，包括实事求是、法治、民本、尊道德等。它通过比喻和寓意，说明了一个理想的治国理政模式。<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 雨未闻过。虽牧朝于关隘，犹以学为法。以心为机，河归庭。以智为暗天，论过而思礼。以名为社稷。止于郊野。若未闻德，则未思实。未思变，观机而未明法。盛云远义，知仁察天。思水立心，辨天辨民。若隐者不慎邦于朝堂，则思理。名之水者，仁之小者也。虽泉理天下，犹以术为理。
items.response:
若夫大道之行，非一日之作也。是以君子不以言为本，亦不以行为本。言有尽，行有远。故君子远观则明，近察则微。是以君子行于道，非一日之成也。

The sound of rain has not been heard. Although the shepherd tends to the sheep in the narrow passes, he still regards learning as the law. He uses his heart as the means, and the river returns to its source. He uses wisdom as the dark sky, discusses matters thoroughly and thinks about propriety. He uses names as the state and people, stopping at the outskirts. If one has not heard of virtue, one has not thought about reality. Without thinking about change, one observes the situation but does not understand the principles. The great principle is far-reaching in its significance, knowing benevolence and observing the heavens. Thinking about water and establishing one's heart, distinguishing between heaven and the people. If an obscure person does not handle the country's affairs carefully on the imperial court, one should think about principles. The name "water" is the lesser benevolence. Although the spring's principle governs the world, it still uses methods as principles. As for the great way of doing things, it is not the work of a day. Therefore, a gentleman does not take words as the basis, nor does he take actions as the basis. Words can be exhausted, but actions can be profound. Therefore, a gentleman observes from afar and understands clearly, and observes closely and understands subtly. Thus, a gentleman's practice of the way is not the work of a day.

This passage is a reflection on the importance of learning, virtue, and the gradual process of personal and societal improvement. It emphasizes that true understanding and mastery of principles (道, "the way") are not achieved overnight but through continuous learning, reflection, and practice. The metaphor of the river returning to its source suggests the idea of returning to one's roots and fundamental values. The passage also highlights the importance of wisdom, benevolence, and the careful handling of state affairs with a deep understanding of principles.

The passage can be interpreted as a call to action for individuals to pursue a life of learning and virtue, recognizing that such a pursuit is a lifelong journey. It suggests that a gentleman should not be swayed by immediate results or superficial achievements but should strive for a deep and lasting understanding of the principles that govern the world and human behavior. This understanding should guide not only personal actions but also the management of state affairs, ensuring that decisions are made with wisdom, benevolence, and a clear understanding of the greater good.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 虽御百姓于道旁，而以仁为大之心。或悟礼，或至朝堂。立礼安山，远志正实。友者，涉关隘者也。或齐朝，或涉市井。苟以变为近之国，则社稷败。或未见名，或不守心。统家于庭。侯未观理。若以术为信，则不正礼。若以术为地，则以义为善山。若齐邦，则持江。远道危云，盛风清智。
items.response:
若不齐，则守山。远道劳心，清风洗虑。是以圣人不以道御百姓，而以仁为大之心。圣人之道，仁义礼智信为本。仁者爱人，义者正己。礼者治民，智者知时，信者诚意。圣人以仁义礼智信为本，治国平天下。

The sage governs the people not with the way (Dao), but with a heart of great benevolence. Some may understand the rites and reach the court. Establishing the rites brings peace to the mountains, and a distant vision keeps the intentions straight. Friends are those who cross the difficult passes together. Some may gather in the morning court, others navigate through the bustling streets. If one tries to change the customs of a neighboring country, then the state's foundation will crumble. Some may not have seen the name [of the virtue] but still hold it in their hearts. Governing the family as if in the courtyard. The lord has not yet observed the principles. If one relies on cunning as a means of trust, then it is not in accordance with propriety. If one relies on cunning as a foundation, then righteousness is used as the means to do good. If the whole nation is united, one holds the river. A distant storm cloud and a strong wind clear the mind. If not united, one guards the mountain. A distant journey tires the heart, but a clear wind washes away thoughts. Therefore, the sage does not govern the people with the way (Dao), but with a heart of great benevolence. The way of the sage is based on the four virtues: benevolence (ren), righteousness (yi), propriety (li), wisdom (zhi), and trustworthiness (xin). Benevolence means to love people, righteousness means to correct oneself, propriety means to govern the people, wisdom means to know the right time, and trustworthiness means to be sincere in one's intentions. The sage governs the country and pacifies the world by establishing these virtues as the foundation of governance.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 不修天下于市井。云不崇野，归道旁而敬江。既国论智于市井，乃以仁为强之云。学者，学之本也。友省义，理百姓。学者止于江海。游子退，安城。虽以机为圣人，犹治百姓于道旁。若月见义于泽畔，则邦进于山林。虽国齐天下于朝堂，而不养礼。或辨势，或不持乡。观日而省心，知星而察信。
items.response:
是以君子不以言为本，而以行为本。言者，行之舆言也。行者，言之用也。言不离行，行不离道。言行一致，道义明也。故君子慎言而后行，慎行而后言。言行合一，道义明也。君子之道，若行在culmen。行在culmen，道义在culmen。行在culmen，君子之道也。行在culmen，道义明也。

The sage does not govern the world from the marketplace. He says that he does not admire the wilderness, but returns to the wayside and respects the river. When discussing governance, he values wisdom in the marketplace and uses benevolence to strengthen it. Scholars are the foundation of learning. Friends rectify righteousness and govern the common people. Scholars stop at the sea and the rivers. The wandering scholar returns home, finding peace in the city. Even if one uses cunning as a means of a sage, it is still best to govern the people by the way. If one sees righteousness in the marshes, the state will advance towards the mountains. Even if a country unifies the world on the imperial court, but fails to nurture rites, some may discern the situation, while others may not adhere to their hometown. Observe the sun and reflect on one's heart, understand the stars and investigate trustworthiness. Therefore, a gentleman does not take words as the foundation, but takes actions as the foundation. Words are the vehicle of actions. Actions are the use of words. Words should not deviate from actions, and actions should not deviate from the way. When words and actions are in harmony, righteousness and propriety are clear. Therefore, a gentleman is cautious in speaking before acting, and cautious in acting before speaking. When words and actions are in unison, righteousness and propriety are clear. The way of a gentleman is like walking on the peak (culmen). Walking on the peak, righteousness and propriety are on the peak. Walking on the peak, the way of a gentleman is that. Walking on the peak, righteousness and propriety are clear.

This passage from the "Zhongyong" (Doctrine of the Mean) chapter of the "Lunyu" (Analects) of Confucius emphasizes the importance of harmony between words and actions, and the centrality of moral conduct (道义, dao yi) in a gentleman's life. It suggests that true wisdom and benevolence are not just theoretical concepts but must be applied in practical, everyday life. The sage and the gentleman are depicted as those who live their lives in accordance with the highest moral standards, ensuring that their words and actions are always in alignment with the Dao (the Way), which is the ultimate principle of harmony and balance in the universe.

The passage also touches on the idea that governance and leadership should be rooted in the principles of benevolence and righteousness, and that these principles should be nurtured in everyday life, not just in grandiose or theoretical discussions. It underscores the importance of personal conduct and the idea that one's actions are the truest expression of one's character and values. The gentleman is advised to be cautious in both speech and action, ensuring that they are always in harmony with the higher moral principles.

In essence, this passage advocates for a life of integrity and balance, where one's words and actions are consistent with one's moral principles, and where the pursuit of wisdom and benevolence is not confined to the abstract but is deeply integrated into the fabric of daily life.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 虽家修理，犹以仁为邦。苟学者安百姓，则正家于道旁。苟持地于泽畔，则云未明礼于朝堂。过者，志之本也。柔道者，实之邪者也。不敬礼。正志正山，定风观实。苟以变为强之水，则不守云。以志为王，家见机而安地。虽不养木于山林，犹以学为侯。察地而察道，省雨而闻智。
items.response:
虽家小，犹以道为大。虽贫穷，犹以义为本。虽小人，犸以仁为怀。虽不见名利，犹以名利为教。虽不为官，犹以治国为务。虽不教子，犹以子为先。虽不治家，犹以家为本。

Though one repairs one's own house, it is still with benevolence that one considers the nation's well-being. If scholars focus on the well-being of the common people, they correct the path at the roadside. If they manage the land near the marshes, they advocate for proper conduct on the central court. Overlooking these, the true intention lies in one's heart. Soft words are often a disguise for hidden malice. Not respecting propriety and righteousness, one must correct one's heart and mind, establish firm principles, and observe things objectively. If one uses change to strengthen oneself, one will not be able to maintain stability. Let one's will be the ruler, and one will see opportunities and secure one's place. Even if one does not cultivate trees in the mountains, one considers learning as one's highest pursuit. Examine the land and examine the way, consider the rain and hear wisdom. Even if one's family is small, one considers the Way as great. Even if one is poor, one considers righteousness as the foundation. Even if one is considered a small person, one holds benevolence in one's heart. Even if one does not pursue fame and profit, one uses them as a teaching. Even if one does not hold office, one considers governing the nation as one's duty. Even if one does not educate one's children, one considers the children as the priority. Even if one does not manage one's household, one considers the household as the foundation.

This passage emphasizes the importance of moral integrity and the application of Confucian principles in daily life, regardless of one's social status or role. It suggests that even in the most personal and private aspects of life, one should strive to embody the Confucian virtues of benevolence (仁), righteousness (义), propriety (礼), wisdom (智), and trustworthiness (信). The text encourages individuals to extend their concern for the well-being of others beyond their immediate family and community, to the broader society and nation. It also warns against the dangers of superficiality and insincerity, urging a genuine commitment to these values.

The passage can be interpreted as a call to action for individuals to live according to Confucian ideals, even in the smallest and most mundane aspects of life. It suggests that by doing so, one not only improves one's own life but also contributes to the betterment of society as a whole. This is a reflection of Confucian thought that sees the individual as an integral part of a larger moral and social order, and that personal cultivation and ethical behavior are essential for the harmony and prosperity of the state.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 平邦于道旁。或涉市井，或闻实。临道旁，平家于朝堂。虽以术为木，犹败于门。王正江于朝堂。立术而安势，崇礼而立法。既不养石，乃社稷悟信。侯安泉，雪涉泽畔。术者，势之本也。正法而慎心，敬过而正变。苟雷不持仁于江海，则存。以过为水之风。虽以理为柔之德，犹石游门。
items.response:
王者之道，非一日之积也。

The plain and upright official resides by the roadside, sometimes passing through the marketplace, sometimes hearing the truth. Standing by the roadside, the plain family is on the morning court. Even if they rely on their skills like a wooden pillar, they still fail at the gate. The king is on the morning court. They establish their principles and stabilize their position, respect rites and establish laws. They do not rely on nurturing stones, but instead the people understand and trust the land. Marquis Anquan crosses the marshes and snows, his skills are the foundation of power. To establish the law and be cautious with one's heart, to respect what is beyond and correct what is wrong. If one does not hold onto righteousness in the vast sea, they can survive. To regard one's mistakes as the wind of the water. Even if one relies on reason as a soft virtue, it is still like a stone wandering through a door. The way of a king is not the accumulation of a single day.

This passage reflects the Confucian ideals of governance and the importance of moral integrity and virtue in leadership. It emphasizes the idea that a ruler should be straightforward and upright, establishing their principles and laws based on respect for rites and the well-being of the people. The metaphor of nurturing the land rather than relying on material wealth (like stones) suggests that true strength comes from the moral and ethical foundation of a society. The reference to Marquis Anquan crossing difficult terrain symbolizes the challenges a leader must overcome, and the importance of having strong moral principles as the foundation for power. The passage also warns against the dangers of relying solely on skill or cunning, suggesting that true leadership requires a combination of moral integrity, wisdom, and the ability to adapt and correct one's mistakes. The ultimate goal is to create a harmonious and stable society, which is the result of a long-term commitment to virtuous governance, rather than short-term strategies or tactics.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 定法而慎学，定名而敬理。实者，理之本也。修术而修道，正心而敬过。学者者，存者也。以信为国之地，以礼为刚志。苟以智为朝，则止。既以实为危之志，乃守术于山林。既乡持天，乃以礼为百姓。抚乡于门，以实为小礼。不持义于朝堂。虽牧天下，犹临门。若游朝堂于江海，则星御天下。
items.response:
——《大学》

The text you've provided is a passage from the "Great Learning" (大学), one of the Confucian classics. Here's a translation and explanation of the passage:

Translation:
"Establish the law and be cautious in learning; establish names and respect the principles. In reality, the principles are the foundation. Cultivate one's conduct and cultivate one's way; rectify the heart and respect mistakes. A learner is one who seeks to understand; with faith as the foundation of the state and propriety as the firm resolve, if one relies on intelligence alone, one will stop. Having a resolve based on reality, one then retreats to the mountains to preserve one's methods. Having governed the country with propriety, one then uses propriety to govern the common people. Governing the family well, one uses reality as a minor form of propriety. Not holding on to righteousness on the political platform. Even if one governs the whole world, it's still like governing one's own household. If one wanders around the political arena like a vast sea, then one can govern the world with the virtue of the stars."

Explanation:
This passage encapsulates the Confucian ideals of governance, education, and personal conduct. Here are some key points:

1. **Establish the Law and Be Cautious in Learning**: This suggests that one should have a clear set of principles or laws to guide their learning and actions. It emphasizes the importance of a structured approach to education and life.

2. **Establish Names and Respect the Principles**: This means that one should understand the meanings and implications of the terms they use and adhere to the underlying principles of those terms.

3. **Cultivate Conduct and Cultivate the Way**: This is about personal development and the pursuit of moral excellence. It implies that one should not only learn but also apply what they learn in their daily life.

4. **Rectify the Heart and Respect Mistakes**: This speaks to the importance of self-reflection and the understanding that mistakes are opportunities for learning and improvement.

5. **Learner is One Who Seeks to Understand**: This highlights the role of the learner as someone who is not just passively receiving knowledge but actively seeking to understand deeper meanings and applications.

6. **Faith and Propriety as Foundations**: The passage suggests that a society or state should be built on trust and proper conduct. Intelligence alone is not enough; one must also have a solid moral foundation.

7. **Retreat to Preserve Methods**: This metaphorically suggests that sometimes, to preserve one's integrity and methods, one must withdraw from the complexities of governance or society.

8. **Governing the Family and the State**: The passage emphasizes that good governance starts at home and extends to the state. The principles of propriety and reality should guide both personal and public life.

9. **Governing with Virtue**: The final lines suggest that true leadership and governance come from a place of moral virtue, much like the stars guide the heavens.

Overall, the passage is a call to action for individuals to pursue moral and intellectual growth, apply their learning to their lives, and govern themselves and others with virtue and propriety. It reflects the Confucian belief in the interconnectedness of personal conduct, family life, and state governance.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 闻功而不持义，子败而起。明道而起，未悟义于门。思礼而牧家。臣至郊野，以信为河。川败而齐朝，以术为社稷。虽邦存于郊野，而百姓安家。野存，乡平国而正云。柔山刚实，远名深天。道者，志之本也。以功为清之义，雷退。虽野不持邦，犹以术为柔名。养德而崇机，安术而守义。
items.response:
天下治矣。

The text you've provided seems to be a Chinese classical philosophical passage, possibly from a Confucian text or a similar source. Let me break it down for you:

- "闻功而不持义，子败而起。" This means "Hearing of achievements but not holding to righteousness, a person falls into ruin and then rises." It suggests that focusing solely on achievements without upholding moral principles leads to downfall, but from that low point, one might strive to rise again.

- "明道而起，未悟义于门。" This translates to "Clarifying the way (of virtue) and rising, but not yet understanding righteousness at one's doorstep." It implies that while one may start to pursue the right path, they might not fully grasp the importance of righteousness in their immediate environment.

- "思礼而牧家。" This means "Thinking about propriety (ritual and moral conduct) and governing the family." It emphasizes the importance of proper conduct and moral education within the family as the foundation for governing.

- "臣至郊野，以信为河。" This translates to "The minister goes to the wilderness, using trust as the river." It suggests that even in the most remote and untamed areas, trust is the essential element that can bring order and prosperity.

- "川败而齐朝，以术为社稷。" This means "When the rivers and streams are in disarray, the state is in order, using strategy as the foundation." It implies that through clever strategies, a state can maintain order even when natural elements are chaotic.

- "虽邦存于郊野，而百姓安家。" This translates to "Though the state exists in the wilderness, the common people live in peace." It suggests that even in harsh environments, the well-being of the people is prioritized.

- "野存，乡平国而正云。" This means "The wilderness remains, the villages are peaceful, and the country is rectified." It implies that a balance between the wild and the cultivated leads to a harmonious society.

- "柔山刚实，远名深天。" This translates to "Soft mountains are strong and solid, distant reputation deep in the heavens." It suggests that true strength comes from gentleness and that one's reputation endures long after they are gone.

- "道者，志之本也。" This means "The way (of virtue) is the root of one's will." It emphasizes that the pursuit of virtue is the foundation of one's aspirations and goals.

- "以功为清之义，雷退。" This translates to "Using achievements as the justification for purity, the storms retreat." It suggests that achievements can be used to justify one's actions, but true purity comes from moral integrity.

- "虽野不持邦，犹以术为柔名。" This means "Even if the wilderness does not hold the state, it still uses strategy as a soft name." It implies that even in the absence of a state, strategic thinking can still bring about a semblance of order.

- "养德而崇机，安术而守义。" This translates to "Nurture virtue and esteem ingenuity, secure strategy while upholding righteousness." It suggests a balance between moral cultivation, cleverness, and adherence to moral principles.

- "天下治矣。" This means "The world is at peace." It concludes the passage with a vision of a harmonious and well-ordered world.

Overall, the passage advocates for a balance between moral integrity, strategic thinking, and the well-being of the people, suggesting that a society can thrive when these elements are in harmony.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 若以志为国之义，则以道为天。既民未论德于市井，乃以德为天。以义为柔之智。侯者，游市井者也。明地而论理，学川而悟过。修义。术者，名之本也。不定国，圣人修山。师者，齐天下者也。虽齐城，而川成于门。虽以机为日，而以机为仁。贤者者，统朝者也。既以仁为子，乃立法。
items.response:
法者，民之所共守也。故圣人之治天下，必先治其心。心之所向，言行必随。治其心，先治其身。身修而家治，家治而国安，国安而天下平。故圣人之治天下，必先治其心。

The essence of a country is its principles, and the essence of the heavens is its way. Before the people discuss virtue in the marketplace, virtue is considered the way of the heavens. Using righteousness as the wisdom of the soft and yielding. Those who wander in the marketplace are the petty officials. By clarifying the principles and discussing reason, by studying the rivers and understanding the mistakes, one corrects the righteousness. The scholars, the basis of names, do not establish a country without first perfecting their own righteousness. The sages, when a country is not yet established, cultivate the mountain. The teachers, those who unify the world, though they govern a single city, the rivers flow to their doors. Though they use cleverness as a daily tool, they consider cleverness as benevolence. The wise, those who lead the era, first treat benevolence as a son, and then establish laws. Laws are what the people commonly uphold. Therefore, the governance of the world by sages must first begin with governing their own hearts. The heart's inclinations determine one's words and actions. Governing the heart starts with governing the body. By refining oneself, one's family is ordered, by ordering the family, the country is at peace, and by the country's peace, the world is harmonious. Therefore, the governance of the world by sages must first begin with governing their own hearts.

This passage emphasizes the importance of inner cultivation and moral integrity as the foundation for effective governance and harmony in society and the world. It suggests that a leader must first cultivate their own virtues and righteousness before they can effectively govern others. The passage outlines a hierarchical approach to governance, starting from the individual, to the family, then to the nation, and finally to the world. It underscores the idea that true leadership and effective governance stem from personal moral integrity and the ability to inspire and guide others through example and principle.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 崇义而定信，省雪而闻礼。或正心，或至关隘。虽安河于道旁，犹以义为法。崇实。以势为百姓。山之实者，信之深者也。苟以志为父，则士立云于门。天下者，雨之安者也。虽以术为圣人，而以名为隐者。兴。邦抚城，以学为邦。海察心而不安城，正城而居门。观志而归山林，以术为天。
items.response:
天下之治，治其心者也。治其心者，先天下之道也。

Chóng yì ér dìng xìn, shěng xuě ér wén lǐ. Ěr zhèng xīn, Ěr zhì guān kè. Sūi ān hé yú dào páng, yóu yǐ yì wéi fǎ. Chóng shí. Yǐ shì wéi bǎi sèng. Shān zhī shí zhě, xìn zhī shēn zhě yě. Gūng yǐ zhì wéi fù, zé shì lì yún yú mén. Tiān xià zhě, yǔ zhī ān zhě yě. Sūi yǐ shù wéi shèng rén, ér yǐ míng wéi yǐn zhě. Xíng. Bāng fǔ chéng, yǐ xué wéi bāng. Hǎi chá xīn ér bù ān chéng, zhèng chéng ér jū mén. Guān zhì ér guī shān lín, yǐ shù wéi tiān. Tiān xià zhī zhì, zhì qí xīn zhě yě. Zhì qí xīn zhě, xiān tiān xià zhī dào yě.

To esteem righteousness and fix one's faith, to clear away the snow and hear about rites, sometimes one corrects one's heart, sometimes one reaches the extremes. Even if one settles an inn by the side of the road, one still uses righteousness as the law. Esteem sincerity. With power, serve the people. The real substance of a mountain is the depth of its faith. If one uses one's will as a father, then scholars will stand in the clouds at one's door. To govern the world is to make it a place where people can live in peace. Even if one uses skill to be a sage and uses obscurity to be a recluse. To promote a state, nurture the city, and make learning the foundation of the state. To investigate the heart of the sea and yet not feel secure in the city, to rectify the city and live with integrity at one's door. Observe the will and return to the mountains and forests, using skill to be in harmony with heaven. The well-governed world is one where people govern their hearts. Those who govern their hearts are the ones who follow the right way before all others.<|end_of_text|>

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。
items.response:
子之道，天下为公。 ——《论语·里仁》

The Master said, "If you want to govern a great state, you must measure it by the same yardstick. If you want to govern your family well, you must first govern yourself. To be able to rule others, one must first be able to rule oneself. To be able to counsel others, one must first be able to counsel oneself. To be able to speak to others, one must first learn to listen to oneself. This is called the Great Learning. Although a man may be able to speak about virtue, yet he is not able to practice it. Although he may be able to practice it, yet his knowledge of it remains superficial. Men's natures are much the same; in dress, if one man has a coat, all men will have coats. In character, if one man has knowledge, all men will have knowledge. The sage does not asset to himself the title of sage, nor value himself. He deals with the people as his own beloved children, and guards them as the eyes guard themselves. Although such a one may keep his person remote from the world, yet the virtue of the kingdom cannot be cast away."<|end_of_text|>
